codeReview.summary: The fix adds a second assertion around UpdateTimeEntry, but it does not actually prove a second supported timer-start entry point. The new test only updates the already-running entry and explicitly asserts that this same-entry update must not conflict, while the service change persists the entry update before checking for a competing running timer. As a result, VAL-REG-001 still lacks evidence that two distinct start paths enforce the same conflict rule/result.

issues.description: The new VAL-REG-001 test never exercises UpdateTimeEntry as a conflicting second start path. It updates the already-running entry (`TimeEntryID: firstEntry.ID`) and asserts that this same-entry update should succeed, so the regression still only proves a conflict on CreateTimeEntry instead of across two supported start entry points.

UpdateTimeEntry writes the entry via `service.store.UpdateTimeEntry(...)` before checking whether another running timer already exists. If a caller updates a stopped entry into a running one while another timer is current, the method can return `ErrRunningTimeEntryExists` after persisting the new running-shaped row, leaving the stored time entry changed even though the conflict was rejected. That does not match the intended 'same conflict rule/result' safety for a second start entry point.

summary: Fail: the fix does not clear the original blocker because the new test does not prove a second conflicting start path, and the UpdateTimeEntry conflict check happens after persistence, so the second entry point is not safely aligned with CreateTimeEntry.
